APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work addresses a core semantic parsing problem (UCCA) with a novel transition-based DAG parser supporting reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals, which is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venue scope. The methodology uses contemporary modeling and evaluation practices (neural classifiers, BiLSTMs, dynamic oracle, Adam, dropout), compares against multiple strong baselines on two UCCA datasets, and reports labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges. Limitations (e.g., discarding inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion-based upper bounds for comparisons) are acknowledged, and hyperparameters are reported, so the paper is well positioned and relevant to the venue audience. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles semantic parsing (UCCA) with a novel transition-based DAG parser that directly addresses linguistic phenomena (reentrancy, discontinuity, non-terminals) of interest to the community. The methodology (neural classifier + BiLSTM features), thorough baselines (including DAGParser, TurboParser, LSTM parser), and standard evaluation (labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges) meet expected standards. While some UCCA phenomena (inter-sentence edges, linkage/implicit nodes) are excluded from training/evaluation, the work still presents the first practical UCCA parser and is positioned well for impact on semantic parsing and downstream applications.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses a core semantic parsing problem (UCCA) with a novel transition-based DAG parser supporting reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals, which is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venue scope. The methodology uses contemporary modeling and evaluation practices (neural classifiers, BiLSTMs, dynamic oracle, Adam, dropout), compares against multiple strong baselines on two UCCA datasets, and reports labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges. Limitations (e.g., discarding inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion-based upper bounds for comparisons) are acknowledged, and hyperparameters are reported, so the paper is well positioned and relevant to the venue audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles semantic parsing (UCCA) with a novel transition-based DAG parser that directly addresses linguistic phenomena (reentrancy, discontinuity, non-terminals) of interest to the community. The methodology (neural classifier + BiLSTM features), thorough baselines (including DAGParser, TurboParser, LSTM parser), and standard evaluation (labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges) meet expected standards. While some UCCA phenomena (inter-sentence edges, linkage/implicit nodes) are excluded from training/evaluation, the work still presents the first practical UCCA parser and is positioned well for impact on semantic parsing and downstream applications.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work addresses an important gap — the first practical parser for UCCA that jointly handles reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminal nodes — which is valuable for semantic parsing. The empirical results (BiLSTM-based TUPA outperforming strong baselines in in- and out-of-domain evaluations on Wiki and 20K Leagues) suggest researchers will adopt its transition set and modeling choices. Some practical limitations (discarding inter-sentence, linkage and implicit nodes, and no mention of released code) reduce immediate broad impact, but the approach clearly opens new directions for DAG-based semantic parsing and downstream semantic tasks. | B(4): This work targets an important gap: the first parser that jointly handles UCCA’s key structural properties (reentrancy, discontinuity, non-terminal nodes), which are linguistically essential and were unsupported by existing parsers. By introducing a novel transition set and coupling it with neural (BiLSTM) feature extraction that yields clear gains over strong baselines on in- and out-of-domain data, the paper meaningfully advances practical UCCA parsing and makes downstream use of UCCA representations more feasible. While some annotation artifacts (linkage/implicit nodes, inter-sentence edges) were excluded, the contribution still opens significant new research directions—robust DAG parsing, cross-framework comparisons, and multilingual extensions—and is likely to be built upon by the semantic parsing community.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses an important gap — the first practical parser for UCCA that jointly handles reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminal nodes — which is valuable for semantic parsing. The empirical results (BiLSTM-based TUPA outperforming strong baselines in in- and out-of-domain evaluations on Wiki and 20K Leagues) suggest researchers will adopt its transition set and modeling choices. Some practical limitations (discarding inter-sentence, linkage and implicit nodes, and no mention of released code) reduce immediate broad impact, but the approach clearly opens new directions for DAG-based semantic parsing and downstream semantic tasks.

reviewer_b.justification: This work targets an important gap: the first parser that jointly handles UCCA’s key structural properties (reentrancy, discontinuity, non-terminal nodes), which are linguistically essential and were unsupported by existing parsers. By introducing a novel transition set and coupling it with neural (BiLSTM) feature extraction that yields clear gains over strong baselines on in- and out-of-domain data, the paper meaningfully advances practical UCCA parsing and makes downstream use of UCCA representations more feasible. While some annotation artifacts (linkage/implicit nodes, inter-sentence edges) were excluded, the contribution still opens significant new research directions—robust DAG parsing, cross-framework comparisons, and multilingual extensions—and is likely to be built upon by the semantic parsing community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the experimental evaluation is solid: the authors compare TUPA to a range of strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, Malt, LSTM parser), report in-domain and out-of-domain results, and present BiLSTM vs MLP variants. Related work appears thorough and the paper explicitly states several limitations (inter-sentence edges, linkage and implicit nodes removed) and reports hyperparameters and training details like dynamic oracle and optimization choices. However, important reproducibility elements are missing or unclear (no mention of released code, random seeds, or computational cost), and the paper does not present comprehensive ablation/sensitivity analyses attributing gains to specific architectural or training choices. These are moderate but addressable gaps, so I rate the work as strong but not fully exhaustive. | B(4): The experiments are solid: in-domain and out-of-domain evaluations, detailed metrics for primary vs remote edges, and comparisons to multiple strong baselines (including DAG- and tree-oriented parsers) that support the claim that TUPA handles UCCA phenomena well. There is some sensitivity comparison across classifier variants (feedforward MLP vs BiLSTM-based representations), but systematic ablations of the transition set or feature contributions are not fully detailed, and conversion-based baselines incur known upper bounds. Related work is covered thoroughly and limitations (inter-sentence edges, linkage/implicit nodes, conversion-induced upper bounds) are acknowledged; hyperparameters are reported though code release is not mentioned, which modestly weakens full reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the experimental evaluation is solid: the authors compare TUPA to a range of strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, Malt, LSTM parser), report in-domain and out-of-domain results, and present BiLSTM vs MLP variants. Related work appears thorough and the paper explicitly states several limitations (inter-sentence edges, linkage and implicit nodes removed) and reports hyperparameters and training details like dynamic oracle and optimization choices. However, important reproducibility elements are missing or unclear (no mention of released code, random seeds, or computational cost), and the paper does not present comprehensive ablation/sensitivity analyses attributing gains to specific architectural or training choices. These are moderate but addressable gaps, so I rate the work as strong but not fully exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid: in-domain and out-of-domain evaluations, detailed metrics for primary vs remote edges, and comparisons to multiple strong baselines (including DAG- and tree-oriented parsers) that support the claim that TUPA handles UCCA phenomena well. There is some sensitivity comparison across classifier variants (feedforward MLP vs BiLSTM-based representations), but systematic ablations of the transition set or feature contributions are not fully detailed, and conversion-based baselines incur known upper bounds. Related work is covered thoroughly and limitations (inter-sentence edges, linkage/implicit nodes, conversion-induced upper bounds) are acknowledged; hyperparameters are reported though code release is not mentioned, which modestly weakens full reproducibility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: a novel transition set and neural/BiLSTM classifier are evaluated on two UCCA datasets against a range of strong baselines with labeled P/R/F for primary and remote edges. Mathematical content is light (no complex proofs), and the provided methodological details (dynamic oracle, optimization, hyperparameters) enable reproducibility in principle. My concerns are limited: comparisons require conversion of graphs to forms other parsers can handle (introducing upper bounds), some UCCA phenomena were discarded (inter-sentence, linkage, implicit nodes), and code release is not mentioned — these weaken the conclusiveness of cross-system comparisons but do not invalidate the main results. Overall the experiments are sound with minor caveats that should be addressed for full reproducibility and fair comparison. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: a transition set and neural classifier (including BiLSTM features) that construct UCCA DAGs with reentrancy, discontinuity and non-terminals, and outperform multiple strong baselines on the Wiki and 20K Leagues corpora using labeled precision/recall/F. The methodology appears sound—appropriate baselines (including converted systems with reported upper bounds), a dynamic oracle, and reasonable training details—however the summary does not report statistical significance testing and notes that some baselines required lossy conversion (introducing evaluation upper bounds). These are minor technical limitations that do not undermine the main experimental conclusions, so the work is largely technically correct.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: a novel transition set and neural/BiLSTM classifier are evaluated on two UCCA datasets against a range of strong baselines with labeled P/R/F for primary and remote edges. Mathematical content is light (no complex proofs), and the provided methodological details (dynamic oracle, optimization, hyperparameters) enable reproducibility in principle. My concerns are limited: comparisons require conversion of graphs to forms other parsers can handle (introducing upper bounds), some UCCA phenomena were discarded (inter-sentence, linkage, implicit nodes), and code release is not mentioned — these weaken the conclusiveness of cross-system comparisons but do not invalidate the main results. Overall the experiments are sound with minor caveats that should be addressed for full reproducibility and fair comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: a transition set and neural classifier (including BiLSTM features) that construct UCCA DAGs with reentrancy, discontinuity and non-terminals, and outperform multiple strong baselines on the Wiki and 20K Leagues corpora using labeled precision/recall/F. The methodology appears sound—appropriate baselines (including converted systems with reported upper bounds), a dynamic oracle, and reasonable training details—however the summary does not report statistical significance testing and notes that some baselines required lossy conversion (introducing evaluation upper bounds). These are minor technical limitations that do not undermine the main experimental conclusions, so the work is largely technically correct.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and presents contributions (TUPA and its capabilities) early, with a clear method→experiments→conclusion flow and thorough related work. Notation and experimental setup (metrics, baselines, hyperparameters) are presented consistently, and figures/tables/examples effectively support the claims about DAG construction and evaluation. A few presentation frictions remain (e.g., more explicit emphasis on discarded phenomena like inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and the conversion upper bounds would improve clarity), but overall the writing is good and understandable. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and communicates its ideas clearly: main contributions are stated early, the transition-based method and neural training details (including hyperparameters and dynamic oracle) are described coherently, and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables (e.g., evaluation breakdowns for primary vs. remote edges) are informative, aiding understanding of UCCA-specific design choices. A couple of presentation wrinkles remain—handling of discarded phenomena (linkage/implicit/inter-sentence edges) and the conversion-based baselines could be explained with slightly more clarity to avoid potential reader confusion. Overall, the writing quality is good with only minor presentation issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and presents contributions (TUPA and its capabilities) early, with a clear method→experiments→conclusion flow and thorough related work. Notation and experimental setup (metrics, baselines, hyperparameters) are presented consistently, and figures/tables/examples effectively support the claims about DAG construction and evaluation. A few presentation frictions remain (e.g., more explicit emphasis on discarded phenomena like inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and the conversion upper bounds would improve clarity), but overall the writing is good and understandable.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and communicates its ideas clearly: main contributions are stated early, the transition-based method and neural training details (including hyperparameters and dynamic oracle) are described coherently, and related work coverage is thorough. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables (e.g., evaluation breakdowns for primary vs. remote edges) are informative, aiding understanding of UCCA-specific design choices. A couple of presentation wrinkles remain—handling of discarded phenomena (linkage/implicit/inter-sentence edges) and the conversion-based baselines could be explained with slightly more clarity to avoid potential reader confusion. Overall, the writing quality is good with only minor presentation issues.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable, concrete novelty in designing a transition-based parser (TUPA) whose transition set and parsing machinery jointly support UCCA-specific structural phenomena—reentrancy (DAGs), discontinuities, and non-terminal nodes—which existing parsers do not handle together. While components like neural classifiers and BiLSTM feature extractors are standard, adapting them with a dynamic oracle and a new transition system for UCCA is a non-trivial methodological extension and a meaningful contribution to semantic parsing. Given the absence of prior work that jointly addresses these UCCA requirements, the contribution is clearly differentiated and would inform experts, though it builds on well-known neural parsing techniques rather than introducing entirely new learning paradigms. | B(3): The paper presents a transition-based DAG parser tailored to UCCA that jointly handles reentrancy, discontinuity and non-terminal nodes — a concrete and useful engineering of transition systems for UCCA’s specific structural needs. However, the core ideas (transition-based parsing, neural classifiers, BiLSTM features, dynamic oracle training) are familiar from prior parsing work, and a closely related prior paper (a 2017 transition-based DAG parser for UCCA) appears to cover very similar ground. Thus the contribution is a meaningful, non-trivial application and adaptation of existing techniques to UCCA, but not a fundamentally new paradigm for semantic parsing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable, concrete novelty in designing a transition-based parser (TUPA) whose transition set and parsing machinery jointly support UCCA-specific structural phenomena—reentrancy (DAGs), discontinuities, and non-terminal nodes—which existing parsers do not handle together. While components like neural classifiers and BiLSTM feature extractors are standard, adapting them with a dynamic oracle and a new transition system for UCCA is a non-trivial methodological extension and a meaningful contribution to semantic parsing. Given the absence of prior work that jointly addresses these UCCA requirements, the contribution is clearly differentiated and would inform experts, though it builds on well-known neural parsing techniques rather than introducing entirely new learning paradigms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a transition-based DAG parser tailored to UCCA that jointly handles reentrancy, discontinuity and non-terminal nodes — a concrete and useful engineering of transition systems for UCCA’s specific structural needs. However, the core ideas (transition-based parsing, neural classifiers, BiLSTM features, dynamic oracle training) are familiar from prior parsing work, and a closely related prior paper (a 2017 transition-based DAG parser for UCCA) appears to cover very similar ground. Thus the contribution is a meaningful, non-trivial application and adaptation of existing techniques to UCCA, but not a fundamentally new paradigm for semantic parsing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares TUPA to a wide and relevant set of strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and an LSTM-based parser) on the same UCCA datasets using the same labeled precision/recall/F metrics and reports primary vs. remote edge scores. The authors are transparent about conversion-induced upper bounds (removal of non-terminals, discarded linkage/implicit/inter-sentence edges) and acknowledge limits of direct comparison, which supports fairness. However, claiming TUPA as the “first” UCCA parser is questionable given closely related prior work on transition-based DAG parsing for UCCA (e.g., the directly relevant 2017 transition-based DAG parser), and the paper does not appear to provide a direct apples-to-apples comparison to that most relevant competing approach, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares TUPA to a range of strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and an LSTM-based parser) on the same UCCA corpora and reports standard labeled precision/recall/F scores for primary and remote edges. The authors explicitly acknowledge that direct comparison is not possible for full UCCA (because baselines require conversion to bilexical graphs/trees, removing non-terminals) and report upper bounds stemming from that conversion, which makes the comparisons candid if imperfect. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and prior approaches that cannot handle UCCA’s joint requirements are discussed, so most relevant competing methods are cited and analyzed. Given the unavoidable conversion-induced limitations, the experimental comparisons are good overall but not fully comprehensive for parsers that natively support UCCA-style structures, hence not a perfect (5) score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares TUPA to a wide and relevant set of strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and an LSTM-based parser) on the same UCCA datasets using the same labeled precision/recall/F metrics and reports primary vs. remote edge scores. The authors are transparent about conversion-induced upper bounds (removal of non-terminals, discarded linkage/implicit/inter-sentence edges) and acknowledge limits of direct comparison, which supports fairness. However, claiming TUPA as the “first” UCCA parser is questionable given closely related prior work on transition-based DAG parsing for UCCA (e.g., the directly relevant 2017 transition-based DAG parser), and the paper does not appear to provide a direct apples-to-apples comparison to that most relevant competing approach, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares TUPA to a range of strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, and an LSTM-based parser) on the same UCCA corpora and reports standard labeled precision/recall/F scores for primary and remote edges. The authors explicitly acknowledge that direct comparison is not possible for full UCCA (because baselines require conversion to bilexical graphs/trees, removing non-terminals) and report upper bounds stemming from that conversion, which makes the comparisons candid if imperfect. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and prior approaches that cannot handle UCCA’s joint requirements are discussed, so most relevant competing methods are cited and analyzed. Given the unavoidable conversion-induced limitations, the experimental comparisons are good overall but not fully comprehensive for parsers that natively support UCCA-style structures, hence not a perfect (5) score.

main_claims: We present TUPA, the first parser for UCCA (Universal Conceptual Cognitive Annotation).

TUPA is a transition-based parser that supports multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes—structural properties required by UCCA that existing parsers do not jointly support.
Coupling a neural-network classifier with BiLSTM feature extraction, TUPA outperforms a variety of strong baselines in in-domain and out-of-domain evaluations.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based parser (TUPA) using a novel transition set to construct DAGs with non-terminal nodes, trained with neural classifiers (feedforward MLP and variants) and BiLSTM-based feature representations; training uses a dynamic oracle, Adam optimization, dropout and word dropout.
datasets: UCCA Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea English–French parallel corpus (20K Leagues)
baselines: Stack LSTM-based parser of Dyer et al. (referred to as LSTM Parser)
metrics: Labeled F-score (reported for primary edges and for remote edges)
limitations: Inter-sentence UCCA edges (0.18% of edges) are discarded for training/evaluation (parsers trained on individual sentences).
Linkage nodes/edges and implicit nodes are discarded in training/evaluation.
No direct comparison with existing parsers for full UCCA is possible; comparisons required conversion (e.g., to bilexical graphs/trees), introducing upper bounds due to removal of non-terminal nodes.
_summary.topic_area: Semantic parsing - UCCA parsing (transition-based DAG parsing)

reviewer_a.name: Pragmatic Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in NLP and 8+ years building and evaluating parsing and structured prediction systems in both academia and industry.
traits: carefully inspects experimental design: baselines, train/dev/test splits, and whether comparisons are fair and reproducible
requires clear ablation studies and controlled experiments to attribute gains to specific modeling choices
looks for implementation and optimization details (hyperparameters, training schedule, random seeds, computational cost) and prefers released code or runnable artifacts
checks evaluation methodology rigor: metric definitions, statistical significance, error margins and coverage of failure cases
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on empirical rigor and reproducibility: accepts claims only when supported by thorough baselines, ablations, and transparent implementation details.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Parsing Strategist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in semantic representations and parsing formalisms (AMR, SDP, UCCA) with a track record of evaluating representation expressivity and downstream utility.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior formalisms and parsers, and whether the proposed contributions close an important gap in the literature
values analyses that connect technical contributions to linguistic/semantic needs (e.g., reentrancy, discontinuity, non-terminals) and examines whether the design genuinely supports them
looks for broader impact: applicability to downstream tasks, multilinguality, and whether the approach advances the field beyond incremental engineering
notices clarity of exposition, examples, and qualitative error analysis that illuminate strengths and weaknesses
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's novelty and significance for the semantic parsing community, emphasizing whether the technical choices meaningfully address UCCA's linguistic requirements and enable new applications.
